flashcards.front: What is the core principle of the SIMD architecture?
flashcards.back: A single instruction is executed simultaneously by multiple processing elements, each operating on its own distinct data item (Single Instruction, Multiple Data).

flashcards.front: What is the role of the Control Processor in a classical SIMD machine?
flashcards.back: It fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, and broadcasts the same instruction to all Processing Elements (PEs) for synchronized, lockstep execution.

flashcards.front: Name one key advantage and one key limitation of SIMD.
flashcards.back: Advantage: High performance and energy efficiency for data-parallel tasks. Limitation: Poor handling of conditional branching (if-else statements) due to lockstep execution.

A modern implementation of SIMD within a processor core, using wide vector registers (e.g., 256-bit) to hold multiple data elements so a single instruction can operate on all of them at once. Examples include Intel's AVX and ARM's NEON.

flashcards.front: How is the SIMD concept applied in GPU programming?
flashcards.back: GPUs execute threads in groups called warps (NVIDIA) or wavefronts (AMD). All threads in a warp execute the same instruction on different data in lockstep, which is an SIMD-like execution model (often termed SIMT).
